section.para: The First Amendment's religion clauses—the Establishment Clause and the Free Exercise Clause—create a dual mandate: the government cannot establish a religion, nor can it prohibit its free exercise. The Supreme Court's efforts to navigate the inherent tension between these two commands have produced some of its most complex and controversial jurisprudence, particularly in the areas of education and the application of neutral laws to religious practices.

title.emphasis: A. The Establishment Clause: Defining the "Wall of Separation"

section.para: The Court has developed several tests to determine whether a government action impermissibly "establishes" religion, with the central debate revolving around the height and permeability of the "wall of separation" between church and state.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
McCollum v. Board of Education **, 333 U.S. 203 (1948)**
section.para: The Court confronted a "released time" program in Champaign, Illinois, where religious teachers came into public school classrooms once a week to provide religious instruction to students whose parents had consented.95 Students who did not participate were required to leave their classrooms and pursue secular studies elsewhere.95 The Court, in an 8-1 decision, found the program unconstitutional.96 It held that the use of tax-supported public school buildings for religious instruction and the close cooperation between school authorities and religious groups constituted a violation of the Establishment Clause, as it utilized the state's compulsory education system to aid religious groups.66

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Zorach v. Clauson **, 343 U.S. 306 (1952)**
section.para: Four years after McCollum, the Court distinguished its earlier ruling. It upheld a New York City "released time" program where students were permitted to leave school grounds during the school day to receive religious instruction at private religious centers.66 The Court, in a 6-3 decision, found this program constitutional because it did not involve religious instruction in public school classrooms or the expenditure of public funds.99 Justice William O. Douglas wrote for the majority that the First Amendment "does not say that in every and all respects there shall be a separation of Church and State," and that to find otherwise would show a "hostility to religion".99

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Engel v. Vitale **, 370 U.S. 421 (1962)**
section.para: In this landmark case, the Court struck down a New York State Board of Regents policy that required public school students to recite a short, non-denominational prayer at the beginning of each school day.66 The prayer was voluntary, and students could be excused. Nonetheless, the Court ruled 6-1 that the practice was "wholly inconsistent with the Establishment Clause".102 Justice Hugo Black wrote for the majority that "it is no part of the business of government to compose official prayers for any group of the American people to recite as a part of a religious program carried on by government".6

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Abington School District v. Schempp **, 374 U.S. 203 (1963)**
section.para: Building on Engel, the Court invalidated a Pennsylvania law that required public schools to begin each day with a reading of at least ten verses from the Bible.66 The Court, in an 8-1 decision, held that the practice constituted a religious exercise and was therefore unconstitutional.105 This case is significant for establishing a crucial test for Establishment Clause violations: "to withstand the strictures of the Establishment Clause, there must be a secular legislative purpose and a primary effect that neither advances nor inhibits religion".105 This two-part test became a cornerstone of the Court's church-state jurisprudence.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Lemon v. Kurtzman **, 403 U.S. 602 (1971)**
section.para: This case involved state laws in Pennsylvania and Rhode Island that provided financial aid to church-related elementary and secondary schools.106 The Court found these laws unconstitutional and, in doing so, synthesized its prior rulings into a comprehensive, three-part test known as the

Lemon test.106 To be constitutional under the Establishment Clause, a statute must: (1) have a secular legislative purpose; (2) its principal or primary effect must be one that neither advances nor inhibits religion; and (3) the statute must not foster "an excessive government entanglement with religion".106 The Court found that the state aid programs failed the "entanglement" prong because they would require ongoing state surveillance to ensure that public funds were not used for religious purposes.106

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Torcaso v. Watkins **, 367 U.S. 488 (1961)**
section.para: The Court unanimously struck down a provision of the Maryland Constitution that required individuals to declare a belief in "the existence of God" as a qualification for holding public office.108 Roy Torcaso had been denied a commission as a notary public for refusing to make such a declaration.109 The Court held that this religious test for public office unconstitutionally invaded freedom of belief and religion. It affirmed that "neither a State nor the Federal Government can constitutionally pass laws or impose requirements which aid all religions as against non-believers, and neither can aid those religions based on a belief in the existence of God as against those religions founded on different beliefs".109

title.emphasis: B. The Free Exercise Clause: The Conflict Between Religious Practice and Neutral Laws

section.para: The Free Exercise Clause protects an individual's right to practice their religion, but this protection often comes into conflict with generally applicable laws that incidentally burden religious conduct. The Court's jurisprudence in this area has seen a dramatic pendulum swing, moving from a standard that required the government to show a "compelling interest" to burden religious practice to one that largely defers to neutral laws of general applicability.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Sherbert v. Verner **, 374 U.S. 398 (1963)**
section.para: Adell Sherbert, a Seventh-day Adventist, was fired from her job for refusing to work on Saturdays, her Sabbath.110 She was then denied unemployment benefits by South Carolina because the state determined she had refused available work without "good cause".111 The Supreme Court, in a 7-2 decision, held that the denial of benefits imposed an unconstitutional burden on the free exercise of her religion.110 The Court established the

Sherbert test: for a state to justify a law that imposes a substantial burden on religious practice, it must show that the law is necessary to serve a "compelling state interest" and is the least restrictive means of achieving that interest.66 The Court found no such compelling interest in this case.

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Wisconsin v. Yoder **, 406 U.S. 205 (1972)**
section.para: Applying the Sherbert test, the Court granted an exemption to Amish parents from a Wisconsin law that required them to send their children to school until age 16.112 The Amish parents refused to send their children to public school past the eighth grade, arguing that high school attendance was contrary to their religious beliefs and way of life.112 The Court found that the state's interest in universal secondary education, while important, was "not of sufficient magnitude to override the interest claiming protection under the Free Exercise Clause".112 The Court was persuaded by the long history of the Amish as a self-sufficient and law-abiding community and their alternative system of informal vocational education.112

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Employment Division v. Smith **, 494 U.S. 872 (1990)**
section.para: This case marked a seismic shift in Free Exercise jurisprudence. Two members of the Native American Church were fired from their jobs as drug rehabilitation counselors for ingesting peyote, a hallucinogen, as part of a religious ceremony.113 They were denied unemployment benefits because their dismissal was for "misconduct" under state law.114 The Supreme Court, in a 6-3 decision authored by Justice Antonin Scalia, upheld the denial of benefits.113 The Court largely abandoned the

Sherbert compelling interest test, holding that the Free Exercise Clause does not relieve an individual of the obligation to comply with a "valid and neutral law of general applicability on the ground that the law proscribes (or prescribes) conduct that his religion prescribes (or proscribes)".66 To allow otherwise, Scalia argued, would make every individual "a law unto himself".113

section.title:
Case Analysis:
Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah **, 508 U.S. 520 (1993)**
section.para: The Court clarified the limits of the Smith decision in this case. The City of Hialeah, Florida, passed several ordinances that prohibited the "unnecessary" killing of animals in a ritual or ceremony, effectively banning the animal sacrifice central to the Santería religion.116 The Supreme Court unanimously struck down the ordinances.116 The Court found that the laws, while appearing neutral on their face, were neither neutral nor generally applicable. The legislative history and the text of the ordinances, with their numerous exceptions for secular animal killings (like hunting and fishing), demonstrated that they were specifically targeted at suppressing Santería religious practices.116 Because the laws were not neutral and generally applicable, the Court applied the strict scrutiny standard, which the city's justifications failed to meet.116
